perf_measurement.derivation: Reverse-engineered by reading the wrapped engine's source (@rotheric/visuals dist/index.js, which bundles tools/watercolor/svg.js) — no path-count/size knob is documented in the package's public README or index.d.ts. zones=2 (engine minimum) + layers=3 (explicit `layers` wins over the randomized `layerProb` draw) fixes emitted <path> count at exactly zones*layers=6. darkening=1 was required in addition — without it, the same zones/layers values produced EITHER 6 or 9 paths nondeterministically, because darkening<1 gates an undocumented duplicate-path-group branch for multiply-blend zones (captured as a durable cross-project learning: rotheric-visuals-darkening-1-silently-doubles). halo=0/splatter=0 remove the two other conditionally-emitted element groups. smoothness=28 (of the engine's 6-40 range) was the dominant remaining lever tuned to hit the byte-size target once path count was fixed.

static_fallback_recapture.selection_method: middle-sized of 3 real samples at the finalized knobs, following S8's own precedent of avoiding an outlier rather than defaulting to the smallest (S8's remediation history: a picked-smallest sample at Phase A's un-pinned knobs was visually washed-out)

generation_cost_node_timing.methodology: Real @rotheric/visuals randomizeParams()+renderSVG() calls, exact production call shape, timed with process.hrtime.bigint(), 50-call JIT warmup then 200 timed calls

real_browser_measurement.tool: project's own @playwright/test-installed headless Chromium, driven directly by a standalone script (NOT the sandbox's separate Playwright-MCP tool, which epic-state.json already records as non-functional for launching a real Chromium session in this exact Linux ARM64 sandbox during S8)
real_browser_measurement.cpu_throttle_method: Chrome DevTools Protocol Emulation.setCPUThrottlingRate, rates 1x and 4x (4x = Lighthouse's own standard 'low-end mobile' multiplier)
real_browser_measurement.swap_detection_result: at both 1x and 4x throttle, the nav banner's backgroundImage already differed from the manifest's known static-fallback encoding on the VERY FIRST post-navigation read -- i.e. generation-to-swap completed faster than the measurement's own polling resolution (a single Playwright evaluate() round-trip). No positive millisecond swap-latency figure was directly observable in-browser; see VQ-S9-010 for the honest limitation this creates.
real_browser_measurement.longtask_observation: main-thread PerformanceObserver longtask entries were present during page load (63-269ms, startup/hydration work) but occurred independent of the already-completed banner swap, consistent with off-main-thread Worker execution (structurally verified in S3/S5, real-browser-confirmed for aquarelle by S8's MV-1/MV-2, not re-verified from scratch here)

cls_measurement.interpretation: aquarelle's CLS sits at/below lego's (a theme with a custom webfont but NO dynamic banner), isolating the small measured CLS to ordinary font-loading rather than the banner swap; both are far below the 0.1 'good' Core Web Vitals threshold. See VQ-S9-012 for a discussion of this comparison's rigor.

honest_limitations: This sandbox has no reachable physical low-end mobile device. The 4x CPU throttle is Lighthouse's own documented 'low-end mobile' proxy multiplier applied via Chrome DevTools Protocol in a real (not emulated-in-software) headless Chromium -- not a claim of real-device measurement.

No positive in-browser millisecond figure for generation-to-swap latency was captured: the swap completed before the first observable read at both 1x and 4x throttle. The 'no measurable jank' conclusion rests on this observation plus the separately-measured pure-Node.js renderSVG timing, not a single unified in-browser stopwatch reading.
The CLS isolation used a cross-theme A/B comparison (aquarelle vs calm vs lego), not a same-theme banner-present-vs-absent controlled comparison; see VQ-S9-012.

retrospective.harder_than_needed: The 'lite preset' path-count target (~6 paths) was not achievable by tuning zones/layers alone: pinning zones=2/layers=3 still produced EITHER 6 or 9 paths nondeterministically. The cause was a third, unrelated-looking render knob (`darkening`) that the package documents only as a 0-1 opacity parameter but that actually gates an undocumented duplicate-path-group branch in the wrapped engine for multiply-blend zones -- found only by reading dist/index.js directly, since neither the README nor index.d.ts mentions it (captured as a durable learning: rotheric-visuals-darkening-1-silently-doubles). Separately, the first version of the real-browser CPU-throttle measurement script had its own bug: it captured a live first-read as the presumed 'static' baseline to detect the swap, which silently failed because in a warm `next dev` session the swap can complete faster than that first read -- the fix was to compute the known static encoding directly from the manifest source (mirroring tests/e2e/banner-decor.spec.ts's own existing convention, which I should have matched from the start).
retrospective.surprised_by: Swap-to-generation latency was too fast to observe directly even under 4x CPU throttle -- the banner had already swapped by the very first post-navigation read at both 1x and 4x. A stronger result than expected, but it means the 'no measurable jank' claim rests on a combination of proxies (browser observation + separate Node.js timing) rather than one directly-observed in-browser millisecond figure -- flagged as a post-impl verification question (VQ-S9-010) rather than overstated as a precise measurement.

decider_caveats.finding: VQ-S9-012: CLS attribution used a cross-theme comparison, not same-theme banner-present-vs-absent
decider_caveats.reasoning: Ceiling argument: CLS is a sum of non-negative contributions, so the banner's contribution cannot exceed the measured total (0.0046), ~20x under the 0.1 threshold. A same-theme A/B could only refine a number already provably passing; it cannot flip the verdict. Aquarelle also measured lower than lego (no dynamic banner), pointing the same direction. Watch item: re-measure if a future theme's banner uses fundamentally different layout mechanics.
